steps.title: Find the interior angle at the $130^{\circ}$ vertex
steps.content: The angle marked $130^{\circ}$ is an exterior angle of the triangle formed by the two transversals and the parallel lines. The interior angle at that vertex is the supplement: $$180^{\circ} - 130^{\circ} = 50^{\circ}$$

steps.title: Identify the angle between the two transversals at line m using co-interior angles
steps.content:
When transversal j crosses parallel lines m and n, the co-interior angles sum to $180^{\circ}$.

The angle at line n (interior to the triangle) is $50^{\circ}$, so the angle j makes with line m is also $50^{\circ}$ by alternate interior angles.

Similarly, transversal k makes an angle $b^{\circ}$ with line n. Since these transversals meet at line m, the interior angle of the triangle at their intersection is the sum of both angles: $$(50^{\circ} + b^{\circ})$$

steps.title: Apply the triangle angle sum to solve for b
steps.content:
The three interior angles of the triangle must sum to $180^{\circ}$:
$$50^{\circ} + b^{\circ} + (50^{\circ} + b^{\circ}) = 180^{\circ}$$
$$100^{\circ} + 2b^{\circ} = 180^{\circ}$$
$$2b^{\circ} = 80^{\circ}$$
$$b^{\circ} = 40^{\circ}$$
We can verify this using the exterior angle theorem: the exterior angle ($130^{\circ}$) equals the sum of the two non-adjacent interior angles: $130^{\circ} = (50^{\circ} + b^{\circ}) + b^{\circ} = 50^{\circ} + 2(40^{\circ}) = 130^{\circ}$ ✓

A is correct.
